def: s.m. = dīćhit, q.v. adj. & s.m. = dīkshit, q.v.; — 2˚ Name of a tribe of Kanaujiyā Brāhmans.

subs: dikhāʼī denā (-ko), To show oneself (or itself, &c.), to come in sight, to appear, be visible or apparent, be seen.

def: v.t. To show; to direct; to indicate, &c. ( = dikhānā, q.v.); to present the appearance of, to make a show of, to pretend.
more: (caus. of ديکهنا) v. a. To show, exhibit, denote, direct. دکهلاٴي دينا dikhlāʼī denā, To appear, be apparent, seem.

def: v.t. (dialec.) = dikh-lānā, q.v. v.t. To show; to direct; to indicate, &c. ( = dikhānā, q.v.); to present the appearance of, to make a show of, to pretend.
s.m. 1˚ = dikhāwā, q.v.; — 2˚ One who shows the way, a guide, conductor.
n. m. 1. See dikhāvaṭ . 2. A guide; conductor; runner ( dauṛāhā ).
v.n. = dikhnā, and disnā, q.v.
more: Rus. v. n. To come in sight ( dikhnā ).
v. n. To look, to appear.

def: v.n. To be seen; to appear; to seem; — v.t. (dialec.) = dekhnā, q.v. v.t. To see, look, look at, behold, view, observe, perceive, inspect, mark, note, consider, look to, weigh well, examine, prove, try; to search, scan; to watch (for); to feel (as the pulse, &c.); to experience, suffer, endure, tolerate, bear the sight of
more: Mah. v. n. 1. To be seen. 2. To seem.
v. a. (dakh.) To see: see ديکهنا dekhnā.
subs: To see, look at, view, inspect, examine, try
v.n. To come into sight, to be seen, to appear, be visible; to be observed; to be experienced, &c.
v.t. To be permitted to see, to get to see; to find out, to ascertain
v.n. To be seen, to be or become visible, to appear; to seem
wety: Urdu verb set دِکْھنا (dikhnā) دیکْھنا (dekhnā) دِکھانا (dikhānā) دِکھوانا (dikhvānā)Intransitive form of دیکھنا .

def: s.f. = dikhāwaṭ, q.v. Sight, view; exhibition, representation; show, display, ostentation, pomp, pageantry; speciousness, pretence, disguise.
s.m. One who looks on, beholder, spectator, viewer, observer.
more: Rus and Wom. n. m. 1. A beholder; observer; spectator. 2. A proficient; connoisseur; judge.
s. m. Beholder, spectator: see ديکهويا.

def: adj. & s.m. Thin, slender; minute; subtle, &c. ( = daqīq, q.v.); hectic (e.g. tap-ě-diq, 'a hectic fever'); indisposed, ailing; troubled, disturbed; vexed, annoyed, irritated, teased, worried, harassed, plagued; — a hectic fever ( = tap-ě-diq); difficulty, trouble, worry, &c.
more: (Heb. and Chald. ) s. m. 1. A hectick fever. 2. Teasing, trouble, &c. See دقت diḳḳat. دق کرنا diḳḳ karnā, To perplex, plague, tease, annoy, incommode, irritate, exasperate. دق هونا diḳḳ honā, To be irritated, &c. دق دار diḳḳ-dār, adj. Troublesome, teasing. دق داري diḳḳ-dār-ī, s. f. Trouble, vexation.
subs: adj. Troublesome, teasing; in trouble, or affliction, or difficulty; distressed, pinched
s.f. Trouble, vexation, worry; distress, poverty, penury; — diq-dārī uṭhānā, v.n. To undergo trouble, &c.; to take trouble, to be at the pains (to, -kī)
v.t. To tease, irritate, annoy, trouble, harass, worry, plague
v.t. To worry or plague to death, to trouble or annoy exceedingly, to pester, torment, &c.
v.n. To be indisposed, be ailing (e.g. t̤abīʻat diq hai); to be irritated, be annoyed, or worried, &c.

ety: for A. دقة, inf. n. of دقّ 'to be slender,' &c.; with amplification of signification
def: s.f. A minute or subtle thing, knotty or nice point; delicate matter; difficulty; abstruseness, intricacy; trouble, perplexity, difficulty, distress; — diligence, close application, industry; accuracy
more: n. f. 1. Difficulty; abstruseness; intricacy; perplexity. 2. A nice point; a delicate affair. diqqat parṭī hai . Difficulty is experienced. diqqat meṅ paṛnā , v. n. To be in difficulties or distress.
s. f. 1. Abstrusity, difficulty, trouble, uneasiness, diligence, close application. 2. Industry, accuracy. 3. A subtile or minute thing.
subs: adj. Understanding subtleties, &c.; penetrating; discerning; quick of apprehension
To fall into difficulties, get into trouble, be in distress.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian دِقَّت (diqqat, “accuracy; attention”), itself from Arabic دِقَّة (diqqa).

def: direct plural of دِقَّت (diqqat, “difficulty; hurdle”)
oblique plural of دِقَّت (diqqat, “difficulty; hurdle”)
s.f. Attack, onset; butting with the horns (as an animal).
more: s. (dakh.) Attack, onset: see حمله ḥamla.

def: v. n. (dakh. intrans. of ديکهنا dekhnā ) To seem, to appear.
n. m. S. दिक् quarter, पाल preserver. The guardian deities of the ten points of the compass.
more: s. m. A guardian deity of one of the ten ( दिश् dis ) regions or points (of the compass: i. e. ब्रह्मा Brahmā, for upwards; अनंत Ananta, for downwards; इन्द्र Indra, for east; अग्नि Agni, for southeast; यम Yama, for south; नैर्ऋत Nairr̤ita, for southwest; वरुण Varuṇa, for west; वायु Vāyu or पवन Pavana, for northwest; कुवेर Kuvera, for north; ईशान Īsāna or शिव Siva, for northeast).

def: s.m. Initiation as the pupil or disciple of some saint or religious guide (vulg. daććhā).
more: n. m. Pālī. dikkhā . See بيعت

def: s.f. Preparation or consecration (for a religious ceremony); a ceremony preliminary to a sacrifice, initiation, dedication; receiving the initiatory mantra or prayer; undertaking religious observances, or engaging in a course of austerities (for a special purpose), devotion; sacrificing, offering oblations, dedicating oneself (to an object, or to a person), self-devotion.

part. adj. & s.m. (f. -ā), Consecrated, initiated; prepared, ready (for a sacrifice, &c.); performed (as the dīkshā ceremony); — one who has received initiation, or by whom the preparatory ceremonies have been observed; a disciple or pupil (of a Brāhman).
s.m. Consecrator, spiritual father, one who initiates, the communicator of the initiatory mantra or prayer.
s.m. Consecrator, spiritual father, one who initiates, the communicator of the initiatory mantra or prayer.
s.m. Heart; mind, soul; spirit, valour (see jī)
more: n. m. Pahlvī, del . Heart; mind; conscience. See جي 3, 5, 6. 1. Yeh hazrat-i-dil haiṅ, jidhar āë udhar āë . Prov. This heart's a king, where'er he sits he sits a king. 2. Dil meṅ āī ko roke so bhaṛvā . Prov. Who speaks not his mind, a pandar you 'll find. 3. Ek tābūt aur kitne murde, kaṭe kaṭāë kyā dil-gurde! Tāl meṅ pīveṅ kālā pānī, rahe, Zafar, nit unkī nishānī . Riddle. Ans. Qalam-dān . Pen-tray. One coffin and so many dead, so many brave ones slain! Black water out of tanks they drink, their tokens all remain. 4. Burā jo dekhan maiṅ chalā, burā na dekhā koë, Jo dil khojā āpnā to mujh sā burā na koë . Kabīr. I went in search of evil men, but none such could I find, I searched my heart and found as me no one so ill-inclined. 5. Gam nahīṅ gar dil-barī se dil ko le jātā hai woh, Pās mere tab to ātā hai jo dil pātā hai woh . Nazīr. I sigh not when my captivator takes my heart away, For co…

adj. Interesting; entertaining; pleasing. dil-chalā, dil-chalī , adj. See jī-chalā. dil-chalī , Illit. n. f. Courage; generosity, etc. dil-chor , adj. 1. Inattentive; heedless; negligent. 2. Ravishing; captivating ( chit-chor ). dil hāzīr h. , v. n. To retain one's presence of mind. dil khush k. , v. a. To divert, amuse, cheer, delight or gladden the heart. dil daryā . Bountiful as the sea; munificent. Luṭāyā bigānā māl, bandī kā dil daryāo . Prov. Another's property I've given up to plunder free, Your humble servant's heart is boundless as the sea. dil-dihī , n. f. 1. Encouragement. 2. Consolation. dil dukhā'nā , v. a. To hurt one's feelings. dil dekhnā , v. a. 1. To look into the heart. 2. To study one's temper; ascertain one's inclinations; to sound one. Jigar kar chāk, qātil dekhtā thā, Jo pūchhā to kahā, 'dil dekhtā thā .' Nazīr. She pierced my heart and into it the murderess looked and peered, I said 'what's this?' she said 'I wished to see which way it veered. dil denā yā lagānā , v. n. 1. To give one's mind to; apply one's self in earnest. 2. ( dil hāth se jānā ). To give or lose one's heart to. 1. Maiṅ ne jo tujh se dil lagāyā, Kambakht! woh kaunsī ghaṛī thī? Wom. Rangīn. When I did fix my heart on you, you wretch! What luckless hour was it that did me fetch? 2. Dil jis se lagāyā woh huā dushman-i-jānī, Kuchh dil kā lagānā hī hameṅ rās na āyā . Wom. Jānī. On whomsoe'er my heart I fixed my foe became, The moment sure was unpropitious for my flame. dil-rubā ; H. man-lubhāū , adj. Heart-ravishing; bewitching. dil-rubā , n. m. A charmer; sweetheart. dil-rubāī , n. f. Allurement; ravishment; fascination. dil rakhnā , v. a. 1. To possess one's heart or affections. 2. To please; gratify; oblige. 3. To comfort; console. dil sā , better pān sā , ( Bot .) Cordate. dil-sard , adj. Cold-hearted; cold; indifferent. dil saṅbhālnā , v. n. To take courage; nerve oneself to an effort. dil-so/?/khtah , Poet. adj. Afflicted; grieved. dil-soz , adj. Affecting; touching; moving; distressing. dil-shikasta , Poet. adj. Broken-hearted; sorely afflicted. dil-fareb ; H. man-mohan , Poet. adj. Heart-alluring; fascinating; bewitching. dil kā bādshāh , Monarch of one's own heart. Ādmī apne dil kā bādshāh hai . Every one is king over his own heart. dil kā lahrī yā maujī , A merry jovial fellow. dil kaṛā yā kaṛvā k. , v. n. To summon courage or resolution; to bear up. Dil kaṛvā kar-ke davā pī jāo! dil-kash ; H. man-levā , adj. Attractive; winning; inviting; engaging; taking. dil-kushā , adj. Heart-expanding; exhilarating; cheering. dil ko lagnā , v. n. 1. To affect or touch one. 2. To approve itself to one; to seem right, reasonable. dil khaṭṭā k. , v. a. lit . to sour one's temper. To displease or offend one. dil ke phapole phoṛnā , v. a. To pay off old scores; take revenge ( jale phaphole phoṛnā ). Maë-khāne bīch jā ke shīshe tamām toṛe, Zāhid ne āj apne dil ke phapole phoṛe . Khān ābrū. The ale-house ent'ring he straightway each glass and bottle smashed, To day the man of God the gangrene of his soul hath squashed. sādā-dil , A simple or simple-hearted person. 1. Dilkī maiṅ sādi thī kambakht ki us se, annā, Na kīya maiṅ ne to māl o dil o īmān dareg . Wom. Rangīn. 2. Dil ki thī maiṅ sādī, jiskā pātī uskā khātī . Prov. So simple was I that I ate up all I could get. dil-gurdā , n. m. Courage ( bahādurī ). dil-gīr ; Rus. dal-gīr , adj. Melancholy. See اُداس 1. dil-gīrī , Illit. n. f. Melancholy; sorrow (اُداسي). dil lagnā , v. n. 1. To be attached to, fond of. 2. To be in love with or enamoured of. Dil lage kī yārī, Darogā! dil lage kī yārī! 3. To be entertained, amused, or pleased. dil-lagī , n. f. 1. Attachment; friendship. 2. Jest; joke; pleasantry. Khānā be-dil-lagī na pachtā thā, Melā ṭhelā koī na bachtā thā . Wom. Shauq. dil lenā , v. a. 1. To take one's heart away. Dil le līyā to ain ināyat hai yār kī, Achchhā hai! ranjisheṅ to gaiṅ bār bār kī . A favor great she hath shewn to me that she my heart hath took, For with it all my oft-felt heart griefs me have clean forsook. 2. To sound or get at one's heart. dil masos-kar rahnā, yā rah jānā , v. n. To wring one's heart and bear in silence. dil mailā k. , v. n. Wom. To take to heart; take on ( udās h .). dil meṅ pāp lānā , v. n. To cherish an improper sexual desire, or lust after one. Suno hamārī bāt, pāp na zarā dil meṅ lāo! Maiṅ to terā dharam-kā putr, tum mātā kahlāo . Rūp Basant. Now hear me, pray! let not this sin within thy heart, Thy step-son now am I, my mother called thou art! dil meṅ dil ḍālnā , v. a. 1. To put one's heart or mind in another. 2. To possess one's heart; to have influence over one. Ḍāl-kar dushman ke dil meṅ dil banā leṅ apnā dost, Par sitam yeh hai ki, zālim, tū hamāre dil meṅ hai . Arshad. A friend to make I fain within my foe's would put my heart, But then alas! thou, tyrant, in my heart hast lot and part! dil meṅ ḍālnā , v. a. To put into one's mind; to inspire. dil meṅ rakhnā , v. n. 1. To keep to oneself; to keep secret. 2. To bear in mind, to recollect. dil meṅ farq h. , v. n. To distrust; suspect. dil meṅ ghar k. , v. a. lit . to abide in one's heart. To take one's heart; win one's affections. 1. Dushman ke dil meṅ ghar karne ko hunar chāhīye . Prov. It needs skill to make a home in the heart of your foe. 2. Kīṛā zarā sā aur woh patthar meṅ ghar kare! Insān woh kyā na jo dil-i-dil-bar meṅ ghar kare . A worm can make its home within a stone, A man then in his mistress' heart a home might own. dil nashīṅ k. , v. a. To fix in the mind; impress upon. dil-vālā , 1. Bold; courageous. 2. Generous; liberal. dil o jāṅ , Heart and soul. dil o jān se , adv. With all one's heart and soul; heartily. dil o dimāg , Mind and heart; loftiness of soul; ambition. dil hī dil meṅ , adv. In one's inmost heart; to one's self; silently. be-dil , adj. 1. Unwilling; averse; dispirited. Be-dil naukar dushman barābar . Prov. An unwilling servant is a foe. 2. Adventurous; reckless ( bahādur ). be-dil , Poet. n. m. A lover; suitor. be-dilī , n. f. 1. Reluctance; unwillingness. 2. Discouragement; dejection. zindah-dil , adj. Sprightly; lively; merry; buxom; gay. murdah-dil , adj. Dull; cold; dead.

subs: dillī-ke dilwālī muṅh ćiknā peṭ ḵẖālī
prov. The inhabitants of Dillī have sleek faces (indicative of opulence), when, in fact, they are starving.

def: s.m. The brain; head, mind, intellect; spirit; fancy, desire; airs, conceit; pride, haughtiness, arrogance; intoxication; high spirits (produced by stimulants, esp. by drinking bhang, &c.); — the organ of smell
more: n. m. 1. Brain; head; mind; intellect; genius. 2. Pride; haughtiness; arrogance. Karūṅ bistār kyā apnī dugānā kī rukhāī kā, Dimāg ā-kar unhīṅ meṅ ṭhus rahā sārī Khudāī kā . Inshā. My bed fellow's high dudgeon how describe? such pride! You'd think the world's pride all was stuffed in her inside. 3. The organ of smell. Zukām se dimāg band hai. dimāg pareshāṅ k. , v. a. To muddle one's brains; to produce a confused state of mind. dimāg jhaṛnā , v. n. 1. To run (the nose). 2. To have one's pride or nonsense taken out of him. dimāg chalnā, yā āsmān par h. , v. n. To be turned (one's head); to be exceedingly proud or haughty. Āj kal unkā dimāg chalā huā hai. dimāg khālī k. , v. a. To beat one's brains to no purpose. Chār ghanṭe dimāg khālī kīyā, magar khāk na samjhā. dimāg khālī h. , v. n. To feel as though the brains were out; to be addled; bored. dimāg-dār ; Wom. dimāg-pīṭī , adj. 1. Proud; …

s. m. 1. The brain. دماغ تازه کرنا dimāg̠ẖ tāza karnā, To be pleased. 2. (in Persian, also, damāg̠ẖ ) Pride, haughtiness, conceit, spirit, fancy. دماغ چڙهنا damāg̠ẖ chaṛhnā, To be proud with excess of wealth. دماغ کرنا damāg̠ẖ karnā, To be vain, proud, haughty; or rather, to act proudly, &c. دماغ هونا damāg̠ẖ honā, To be vain, haughty, proud.
subs: (kā), To confuse or disturb the brain, to produce a confused state of mind
(-kā), To have a running of the nose; — to have the conceit or pride, &c. taken out (of one)
damāg̠ ćalnā or ćalā-huʼā honā (-kā). To have the head turned, to be or become conceited or proud (with excess of wealth, &c.)
(apnā), To beat or rack the brains
'The head to become empty'; to become light-headed, to be or become confused or stupid, to be worried, be bored
adj. Fanciful; conceited, proud, haughty, arrogant; disdainful, supercilious
To put on airs, to be conceited or vain; to act proudly or haughtily, to be proud, or arrogant
(-ke), To have disorder of the brain, to be deranged
(-kā), To be unable to fathom the pride, &c. (of); to be unable to satisfy the (demands of one's) extreme pride (e.g. uskā damāg̠ nahīn miltā, 'His pride cannot be fathomed,' 'his pride is extreme')
(-ko), To be vain, or proud, or haughty.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian دِمَاغ (dimāġ), borrowed from Arabic دِمَاغ (dimāḡ), a blend of دَم (dam) + مُخّ (muḵḵ).

def: s.m. Day (of twenty-four hours); a day (of twelve hours); daytime; time; circumstances; lot, fate; — (pl.) days, period of life, age
more: n. m. S. दिन, Pālī, dinam . 1. Day. 1. Ek din ke tīn sau sāṭh din haiṅ . Prov. One day my turn will come in days three hundred and sixty. 2. Din dūnī, rāt chaugnī! Prov. Saying. May your days be twice as long and your nights four times as long! (A blessing or a curse). 3. Abhī kaë din, kaë rāt? Prov. How many days and nights have yet passed over you? (It was but the other day). 4. Din īd, rāt shab-barāt . Prov. Each day was festive Id , and every night was Shab-barat (festive night). 5. Kabhī ke din baṛe, kabhī kī rāt . Prov. Now lengthens out the day, and now the night (The inconstancy of time.) 6. Woh din gaë, jo Khalīl Khāṅ fākhtah mārte the . Prov. The days are gone when Khalīl Khān shot doves. (Said to a do-nothing who plumes himself on the deeds of his ancestors). 7. Aiyām musībat ke to kāṭe nahīṅ kaṭte, Din aish kī ghaṛyoṅ ke guzar jāte haiṅ kaise? Shahīdī. The day of sorrow will …

s. m. Day. دن بدن din ba-din, Day by day. دن بهرني din bharne, expresses time spent in pain and trouble. دن پڙنا din paṛnā, To be unfortunate. دن پهرنا din phirnā, To begin to prosper (after adversity). دن چڙهانا din chaṛhānā, 1. To commence any business late in the day. 2. To eat the bread of idleness, or earn oneʼs wages without labour. دن چڙهنا din chaṛhnā, 1. The day being far advanced. 2. The period of a womanʼs menstruation being protracted. دن دهولي din dhaule, adv. In daylight. دن ديا din diyā, Daylight. دن ڐهلنا din ḍhalnā, v. n. To grow late, to decline (the sun). دن کاٿنا din kāṭnā, To pass time with difficulty. دن کودن رات کورات نجاننا din ko din rāt ko rāt na jānnā, To be totally absorbed in thought or business. دن کهلنا din khulnā, To begin to prosper after adversity. دن گنوانا din gaṅwānā, To spend carelessly, or trifle away, time. دن مندنا din mundnā, To grow late, to clo…

A rāg , or musical mode sung at evening. 3. An air sung at dusk; an evening song, or hymn. dīpak chhand , A verse of four lines, each of 10 instants.
s. m. 1. A light, a candle, a lamp. 2. A kind of firework. 3. Name of a rāg, or musical mode, sung at noon in the hot weather.

def: s.f. = diwālī, q.v. s.f. (lit. 'A row of lamps'), A Hindū festival celebrated on the day of the new moon of Kārttik in honour of Kārttikeya, the god of war. (At this festival, the Hindūs, after bathing in some river, put on their best attire, and perform a śrāddh; and at night they worship Lakshmī; their houses and streets are illuminated, and the night is spent in gambling.)

Raṅg ba-raṅg ek panchhī banā, chhoṭī choṅch arū kāṭe ghanā, Tīs tīs mil bil meṅ base, jīv nahīṅ arū uṛ-ke ḍase . Riddle. Ans. Tir . Arrow. To declare bankruptcy; to become a bankrupt. Juā khelne kā jo chaskā paṛā, divālā nikālā divālī meṅ jā! def: s.f. (lit. 'A row of lamps'), A Hindū festival celebrated on the day of the new moon of Kārttik in honour of Kārttikeya, the god of war. (At this festival, the Hindūs, after bathing in some river, put on their best attire, and perform a śrāddh; and at night they worship Lakshmī; their houses and streets are illuminated, and the night is spent in gambling.)
more: n. f. S. दीपाली A festival observed by the Hindus in honor of Lakshmī on the 15th Kārtik . Their houses are illuminated and the night is spent in gambling. 1. Divālī kā dīyā chāṭ kar jāëṅge . At Divāli the snakes will lick the oil-lamps and creep into their holes. 2. Divālī kī rāt ko būṅṭī būṅṭī pukā/?/tī hai . On the night of Divalī every blade and leaf doth find a tongue. 3. Gūlar kā phūl, pīpal kā madh, ghoṛe kī jugālī Kabhī na pāve, aur pāve to rain divālī . Prov. The Gular's blossom, Pipal juice, and a horse that chews the cud None ever saw, or only on Divali night displayed.
wety: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀤𑀻𑀯𑀸𑀮𑀻 (dīvālī), 𑀤𑀻𑀯𑀸𑀯𑀮𑀻 (dīvāvalī) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-), from Sanskrit दीपावलि (dīpāvali), दीपावली (dīpāvalī). Doublet of دِیپاوَلِی (dīpāvalī).

def: s.f. = diwālī or duʼālī, q.v. s.f. (lit. 'A row of lamps'), A Hindū festival celebrated on the day of the new moon of Kārttik in honour of Kārttikeya, the god of war. (At this festival, the Hindūs, after bathing in some river, put on their best attire, and perform a śrāddh; and at night they worship Lakshmī; their houses and streets are illuminated, and the night is spent in gambling.)
more: s. f. A Hindū festival (celebrated on the day of the new moon of kārtik: when the Hindūs, after bathing in the Ganges, or other river, put on their best attire, perform a srāddha, and at night worship Laksh̤mī. The houses and streets are illuminated all night: and, in Hindūstān, the night is universally spent in gaming).
s. m. A temple of idols.
(for دوال see دوالي dūʼālī ) s. f. A leather strap, a belt.

ety: said to be orig. dev-ān, 'an assembly of kings or great men'; and prob. fr. Zend dīv = S. दिव; A. dīwān, fr. the Persian
def: s.m. A royal court; a tribunal (of justice or revenue); a council of state, senate, divan; a court or hall of audience; — a minister, a chief officer of state; finance minister, head of the revenue or financial department; a secretary; a steward; — a complete series of odes or other poems by one author running through the whole alphabet (the rhymes of the first class terminating in alif, the second in be, and so on); — the collected writings of an author

s.m. An old and popular Prākrit and Hindī metre; a couplet, distich (the two verses of which rhyme: each verse consists of 24 mātrās, which are distributed into feet of 6+4+3 mātrās respectively, with a caesura at the end of each first hemistich).
more: s. m. A couplet, distich (in Braj. Sans. द्विपथ ).

def: n. m. S. द्विपदा A couplet; a verse of two lines, of 24 instants each, divided into two charns and six feet, as follows 6 + 4 + 3, 6 + 4 + 1. The last foot (3) in the first charn must be a tribrach (/?/) or an iambus (/?/—), not a troches (—/?/). The last syllable of each line must be short. This is at present the most popular and common of all metres. It is much used by Tulsī Dās, Kabīr, and all the most esteemed poets. Kellogg .

s.m. A house with a double roof; a thatched roof sloping in two directions
adj. Having two compartments, &c.; of two stories ( = du-manzila)
s.m. A kind of ḥuqqa-snake with two bends (such as that of a guṛguṛī)
adj. & s.m. Having two naps (as cloth); — a kind of double-napped cloth
(fr. dām, 'thread'), s.f. Flowered muslin (the flowers being worked with a needle); — du-dasta, adj. (f. -ī), Two handed; having two handles; consisting of, or divided into, two branches or divisions
adj. lit. 'A two-days' guest'; temporary, transitory, fleeting, passing away (as beauty, &c.)
v.n. To die young, to die soon
s.m. A road dividing into two, or branching off in two directions; the junction of two roads

adj. (f. -ī), Double-faced ( = du-bhesiyā); having the same face or appearance on both sides, not having a wrong side; — having two faces or aspects, having the two sides of different colours (as cloth, &c.)
s.m. A soil consisting of clay and sand
s.m. A mixture of two kinds of things (as of tobacco, &c.)
du-rāsta, adj. Having a row of trees, or of houses, on both sides (a road)
adj. & s.m. Capricious, whimsical; — half-bred; — a mongrel, a person of double breed (having good qualities on one side, and bad on the other, so as not to be depended on; syn. doglā)
du-rangā, adj. (f. -ī), Of two colours; piebald; — capricious; double-dealing; hypocritical; — s.m. A capricious person; a double-dealer; a hypocrite
(or do-) rangī, s.f. The property of having two colours; capriciousness; double-dealing, duplicity, deceit, hypocrisy
adj. & s.m. Two-faced, double-faced, hypocritical; — a double-faced person, a hypocrite; — name of a flower (one face of which is yellow and the other red; syn. gul-ě-raʻnā)
adj. & s.m. Consisting of, or lasting for, two days; short, brief; the space of two days
s.f. The having two faces, double-facedness; duplicity, &c. ( = du-rangī, q.v.)
adj. & s.m. Double-faced, &c. ( = do-rū); — adv. On both faces, or sides
(or do-) zānū baiṭhnā or ho-baiṭhnā, v.n. To kneel and sit on the heels behind; to sit on the hams or haunches
s.f. The being forked-tongued; the having two tongues, duplicity; equivocation
v.n. To pierce through and through, to go right through
adj. Of two years, two years old; biennial; — s.m. A two-year old colt; — lands that have been two years under cultivation
do-sāʼī, adj. & s.f. Yielding two crops a year (land; = du-fas̤lī)
s.f. The ploughing land twice; twice-ploughed land
s.m., or du-sūtī, s.f. A kind of cloth, the threads of which are double
s.m., or du-serī, s.f. A weight of two sers
adj. & s.m. Having two branches; forked (a branch); — the fork of a branch
s.m. A pair of shawls (worn like the du-paṭṭā); — du-shāle-posh, s.m. One who wears a du-shālā
adj. Double-bolted (as a lock); double-barrelled (as a gun); inflicting two blows, or a double wound
adj. (f. -ī), Having two sides, two-sided; on both sides, mutual, taking or pretending to take both sides in a dispute, deceitful
s.f. Double rule, the rule of two masters
adj. & s.f. Yielding two crops or harvests in the year (viz. the rabīʻ or spring harvest and the ḵẖarīf or autumnal harvest); — land yielding two crops in the year
v.t. To divide into two parts, to cut in two, to bisect
adj. Two-personed; fit for two persons
s.m. A double padlock, iron chains for the legs of criminals
adj. & s.m. Two-hunched; — a two-hunched camel, the Bactrian camel
(or do-) gāṛā, adj. & s.m. (f. -ī), Twice, double; — a musket that carries two balls, a double-barrelled gun
adj. & s.m. Double, two together; of two sorts; — two genuflexions in prayer; a prayer in which two genuflexions are made; — two intimate friends, an inseparable pair
(or ḍo-) gun, du-gunā, adj. (f. -ī), Double, two-fold, twice as much

(or do-) ganḍī ćiṭṭī, s.f. A go-between, a fomenter of quarrels, one who flatters both parties in a dispute (the expression is taken from a game in which ćiṭṭī or tamarind seeds are used as dice, the seed being marked or rubbed on one face or side; and if it be fraudulently marked on both sides, it is called du-ganḍī)
s.m. Two vessels full of water placed one on top of the other (to pass a do-ghaṛ on the right hand is regarded as a good omen)
s.f. A space of two hours
adj. & s.m. Having two ropes; — a well with two lāʼos or ropes, and buckets
s.f. A wrap made of two kinds of stuff, one sewn upon the other; a quilted covering or robe, a double sheet
s.f. A kicking up, or a kick, with both hind legs (of a horse, or other quadruped)
du-lattī mārnā, To kick up or out with the two hind legs (a horse, &c.)

du-laṛā, and dolṛā, adj. & s.m. (f. -ī), Having two strings or bands, of two rows; having two strings to one's bow; acting a double part, going from one party to another; — a necklace, or girdle, &c. of two strings or bands; — a piece of cloth consisting of two breadths
du-lohī, adj. & s.f. Composed of two plates of steel; — a scimitar made of two plates of steel joined together
du-maṭ, adj. & s.m. Consisting of two soils; — a mixture of two soils
s.m. Name of a subdivision of the Brāhman class
du-manzila, adj. Of two floors or stories (a house, &c.); of two decks (a ship)
adj. & s.m. Having two mouths; two-faced, deceitful; — a two-faced or deceitful person; a kind of serpent with two mouths
v.n. To laugh and amuse oneself in moderation
(or do) nālī, vulg. do-nalī, adj. Double-barrelled (gun)
adj. Having two points or tips; (in Bot.) bifid
adj. & s.m. Divided in two, halved; — two halves
adj. & s.f. Consisting of two leaves; — a book of two leaves or pages, a small book
du-hatthar, adj. & s.f. Held with both hands; — a slap or blow with both hands at one time
s.f. A sword that is held with both hands
v.t. To strike a blow with a sword held with both hands
(-ko), To strike (one) with both hands at one time
